-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44257316:**

-- **Title:** 'General Education Students  Perspectives on Their Involvement in a High School Peer Buddy Program.'

-- **Abstract**: 'A study investigated the perceptions of general education high school students who participated in a structured peer buddy service learning program designed to increase their social interactions with students with severe disabilities. Participants reported having positive attitudes toward their peers with severe disabilities and experienced personal growth. (Contains references.) (Author/CR)'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The study clearly involves a structured peer buddy service learning program in a high school setting, so criteria A (service learning focus) and B (K–12 participants) are satisfied. However, the abstract only states that the study "investigated the perceptions" of general education high school students and that participants reported positive attitudes and personal growth. It does not specify whether data were collected and analyzed using quantitative methods (e.g., surveys with numerical scales, statistical analyses) versus purely qualitative methods (e.g., open-ended interviews, thematic analysis). Because the quantitative nature of the evaluation cannot be determined from the abstract, there is insufficient information to confidently include or exclude based on criterion C. Therefore, the appropriate decision is uncertain (1.1), pending access to the full text or more methodological details.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 1

-- **Answer (Human)**: 1

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44256228:**

-- **Title:** 'The Rural Challenge: Rural Schools and Communities Working as Partners for the Future of Rural America.'

-- **Abstract**: 'The Annenberg Rural Challenge locates and funds exemplary rural public school programs to serve as models for rural education reform. The guiding theory of the Rural Challenge is that when rural schools base their teaching on the economy, ecology, history, and culture of their communities while fully engaging the community in the school s work, schools and communities improve together. A 4-year evaluation of the effectiveness of the Rural Challenge s reform work concluded that: (1) place-based education increased student appreciation of their histories and communities and increased community participation and resources; (2) participation in community-based work instilled valuable new skills in addition to academic understanding; and (3) learning in the community involved demonstrating student leadership, responding to community needs, and contributing to community well-being, and changed expectations of what it means to attend school. Although standardized test results were not a factor in the evaluation, it was noted that Rural Challenge schools demonstrated above-average performance on traditional benchmarks of test scores, attendance, and graduation rates. Examples are given of student projects based on environmental issues, local history and culture, rural economic development, and technology. (TD)'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The abstract clearly involves rural K–12 public school students engaged in community-based, place‑based education that includes responding to community needs and contributing to community well‑being, which is consistent with many definitions of service learning, although the term “service learning” is not explicitly used. Thus criteria A (service learning–type intervention) and B (K–12 students) are likely met. However, the description of the 4‑year evaluation does not specify whether the study used quantitative methods (e.g., experimental, quasi‑experimental, or pre‑post quantitative outcomes). It mainly reports general conclusions and notes that standardized test results were not a factor in the evaluation, with only a brief statement that Rural Challenge schools had above‑average performance, without details on data, measures, or design. Because the abstract does not clearly indicate that this is a quantitative evaluation study, there is insufficient information to confidently determine if criterion C is met. Therefore, the appropriate decision at the title/abstract stage is uncertain (1.1), warranting retrieval of the full text for clarification.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 1

-- **Answer (Human)**: 1

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44258572:**

-- **Title:** 'The relationship between service-learning participation and high school students  achievement in math and science education'

-- **Abstract**: 'This study examined a causal relationship between service-learning participation and academic achievement of 9th grade students in math and science courses. The central purpose of the study was to examine the impact of service-learning participation on students’ academic outcomes, and to determine if this impact was consistent after controlling for the socioeconomic status (SES) of the student. The participants in this study were from the base year data of the High School Longitudinal Study of 2009 (HSLS:09). For this study, 15,058 students identified as being enrolled in mathematics and science classes were selected. Quantitative methods were used to analyze cross-sectional data from the HSLS:09 study. The initial findings from this study suggest that service-learning participation was consistent in student’s academic achievement of all students regardless of socioeconomic status.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Include. The study explicitly focuses on service-learning participation (criterion A), involves 9th grade high school students (K–12 population; criterion B), and uses quantitative methods to examine causal relationships between service-learning and academic achievement using HSLS:09 data (criterion C). Therefore, it meets all inclusion criteria for the review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 1

-- **Answer (Human)**: 1

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44259155:**

-- **Title:** 'The effects of implementing a curriculum-based service learning program incorporating a choral reading model'

-- **Abstract**: 'As the 21$\rm\sp{st}$ century becomes a reality, the challenge for new approaches for revitalizing curriculum and instructional delivery becomes more vital. We are focused to realize the impact that the current negative climate, coupled with tight and dwindling funding, have had on our public educational system. With these thoughts in mind, this study examines the impact of a curriculum-based service learning program for middle school students. A Choral Reading model for reading and communications classes was implemented for eighth grade students with the purpose of examining its impact in the area of improved speech and oral communication skills. These are areas which appear to be lacking in today s youth. The study also investigated evidence of improved cognitive learning and greater interest in poetry and literature. Whether an intergenerational program produced positive change in attitudes, views, and relationships of young adolescents towards senior citizens was also explored. This research study provided findings that indicated service learning is a viable curricular method which increase and encourage community support of public schools. It also showed that integrated into the curriculum, the Choral Reading Model is a successful instructional intervention for teaching both poetry and public speaking skills in reading and communications classes. Service learning is a context-based curricular intervention and is global in nature. The methodology for evaluating service learning initiatives in the past has been primarily qualitative. In this study, seven different qualitative and quantitative research measures were used and all of the findings showed strongly significant results. Three quantitative measures yielded statistically significant positive change. The t-ratios for the Rating Scales for the Oral Reading of Literature indicated significant improvement in all six categories evaluated (intelligibility, vocal loudness, fluency, tempo, intonation, and interpretive presentation) as well as in overall ability. The statistical analysis of the Written Attitude Scales and The Measure of Knowledge in Aging yielded evidence of strong positive attitude change on the part of the students. Qualitative assessments included student journal writing, student interviews, and elite interviews. The process of open and axial coding of this data indicated strong positive change. The Checklist of Personal Gains served to act as a bridge to connect, amplify, and corroborate the data from the other six assessments by relating the constructs from the qualitative assessments to the scores from the quantitative tests using the items on the Checklist which received the most responses.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Include. (A) The study clearly focuses on a curriculum-based service learning program, explicitly described as a service learning intervention using a Choral Reading model in middle school reading and communications classes. (B) Participants are eighth grade middle school students, which falls within K–12 education. (C) The report is a quantitative evaluation study: it describes using multiple quantitative measures (e.g., rating scales, written attitude scales, knowledge tests), reports t-ratios and statistically significant results, and combines these with qualitative data. Therefore, it meets all inclusion criteria for the review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 1

-- **Answer (Human)**: 1

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44258863:**

-- **Title:** 'Service-learning and civic efficacy among youth with disabilities'

-- **Abstract**: 'The concept of civic-efficacy, as well as its role in post-school outcomes for high school students with disabilities is explored. Service-learning is introduced as a possible intervention for improving the civic-efficacy of high school students with disabilities. The existing literature regarding civic engagement, service-learning, and students with disabilities is critiqued. The results of a mixed methods study with quasi-experimental design analyzing the impact of service-learning activities on the civic-efficacy of youth with disabilities are presented. The findings indicate a significant difference in the civic-efficacy scores of youth who participate in service-learning activities. Qualitative analysis revealed that all the youth participating in the focus groups would like to do more service-learning activities and none of the service-learning sites aligned their projects with the service-learning best practices.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The study clearly focuses on service-learning as an intervention and analyzes its impact on civic efficacy. Participants are high school students with disabilities, which falls within the K–12 range. The design is a mixed methods study with a quasi-experimental component, reporting quantitative outcomes (significant differences in civic-efficacy scores), so it is a quantitative evaluation study (with additional qualitative elements). Therefore, it meets all inclusion criteria and should be included.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 1

-- **Answer (Human)**: 1

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44260814:**

-- **Title:** 'Positive peer group interventions: An alternative to individualized interventions for promoting prosocial behavior in potentially disaffected youth'

-- **Abstract**: 'Introduction: Most approaches to reducing the socially inappropriate behavior of adolescents target the individual rather than a group. Evidence suggests greater efficiency and long-lasting effects may be achieved when groups of peers work together to make meaningful contributions to their communities through service learning projects. In the systems-intervention program presented here, titled Positive Peer Group, interventions are implemented with the goal of developing enhanced skills, increasing personal responsibility, increasing students  bonding to their school community, developing an ability to manage conflict responsibly, identifying with positive peers, and increasing social skill repertoires. Method: The subjects were 198 students attending 17 schools with matched controls in Ohio, USA. Prior to entry into the program students exhibited characteristics signaling a capacity for leadership whether as positive role models or as potential leaders of a disaffected group likely to engage in negative behavior patterns. Students experiencing the experimental condition were offered opportunities to work together in small, heterogeneous groups on projects that contributed to improving their school or solving a problem that they themselves recognized within their school or community. Results: Self-report measures taken pre- and post-intervention confirm statistically significant improvements in behavioral accountability, bonding to school, anger management, and the establishment of a psychological sense of school membership—all primary goals for this intervention. Discussion and Conclusion: These data strongly support the contention that increased affiliation with the life of the school established through service-learning enhances social skill development and may inoculate children from becoming further disaffected. (PsycINFO Database Record (c) 2016 APA, all rights reserved)'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Include. (A) The abstract explicitly describes the intervention as involving service learning / service-learning projects where students work together in small groups on projects that contribute to improving their school or solving recognized school/community problems; this matches the definition of service learning. (B) Participants are 198 students in 17 schools in Ohio, USA, characterized as adolescents and students, implying K–12 school-aged youth (primary/secondary education); no indication that they are beyond secondary level. (C) The study reports a quantitative evaluation: it uses self-report measures taken pre- and post-intervention, with matched controls, and reports statistically significant improvements in specified outcomes. Therefore all three inclusion criteria are met.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 1

-- **Answer (Human)**: 1

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44249709:**

-- **Title:** 'Improving Work Performance for Adolescents with Emotional and Behavioral Disorders: A Comparison of Two Work-Based Learning Interventions.'

-- **Abstract**: 'This study compared two work-based learning interventions, service learning and paid internships, across group and time on work performance and social competence among high school students with emotional and behavioral disorders (N=57). Results found a significant interaction effect for group and time, favoring service learning, on several dimensions of work performance and social competence, including work motivation, personal presentation, overall social competence, peer relations, and school adjustment. [ABSTRACT FROM AUTHOR] Copyright of Journal of Rehabilitation is the property of National Rehabilitation Association and its content may not be copied or emailed to multiple sites or posted to a listserv without the copyright holder s express written permission. However, users may print, download, or email articles for individual use. This abstract may be abridged. No warranty is given about the accuracy of the copy. Users should refer to the original published version of the material for the full abstract. (Copyright applies to all Abstracts.)'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Include. (A) The study explicitly evaluates a service learning intervention (compared to paid internships). (B) Participants are high school students, which falls within K–12. (C) The abstract describes a comparative evaluation with statistical results (interaction effects across group and time), indicating a quantitative evaluation study. Therefore, all inclusion criteria are satisfied.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 1

-- **Answer (Human)**: 1

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44253703:**

-- **Title:** 'A Review of Current Methods to Develop Empowering Service-Learning Programs for Latina/O College Students'

-- **Abstract**: 'This review of research suggests a need for service-learning programs that empower Latina/o students. Research on the status of Latina/os in higher education and key challenges to Latina/o student success highlights the demand for innovative programmatic solutions. A review of postcolonialist educational and Latina/o student identity theory along with case studies from critical service-learning programs is presented to provide a framework for program innovation, and recommendations are made for future program development research.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. Although the article focuses on service-learning, it does not meet two key inclusion criteria. First, the population is Latina/o college students in higher education rather than K–12 students. Second, the article is described as a review of research and theoretical frameworks with case studies used illustratively; it does not present a quantitative evaluation of an intervention. Therefore, it fails criteria B (K–12 participants) and C (quantitative evaluation study).

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44409847:**

-- **Title:** 'The association between geographical factors and dental caries in a rural area in Mexico'

-- **Abstract**: 'The aim of this study was to investigate the association between markers of oral disease and geographical factors influencing access to dental care (DMFT score) among school children in Central Mexico. Retrospective data were collected during an international service-learning program between 2002 and 2009. A sample of 1,143 children (55% females; mean age 12.7 +/- 13.1years) was analyzed. The mean DMFT score, represented largely by untreated tooth decay, was 4.02 (4.76). The variables that had the most significant effect on the DMFT score were proportion of paved roads between the community and dental services, and the availability of piped potable water. The DMFT score increased in proportion to the percentage of paved roads. In contrast, the DMFT score decreased with the availability of piped potable water. Similar results were found for untreated tooth decay. The main variable associated with a significant increase in dental fillings was proportion of paved roads. Together with Brazilian reports, this is one of the first investigations of the association between geographical factors and oral health in an underdeveloped setting.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. Although data were collected during an international service-learning program and participants are school children (K-12 age range), the focus of the study is not on evaluating a service-learning intervention. Instead, it is an observational study examining the association between geographical factors (paved roads, potable water) and dental caries (DMFT scores). There is no quantitative evaluation of the effects or outcomes of a service-learning intervention on students, which is required by the inclusion criteria.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44259098:**

-- **Title:** 'Impact of service learning on occupational therapy students  awareness of, commitment to, and sense of responsibility toward community'

-- **Abstract**: 'Health care is being called upon to better prepare practitioners for providing health related services within the community. This dual design study looked at the value and impact of using service learning as pedagogy to improve occupational therapy students  awareness, commitment, and sense of responsibility toward community. Sixty-nine occupational therapy students from two occupational therapy practice courses participated in the study, with 44 students in the experimental group and 25 in the comparison group. The experimental group participated in service experiences of their choice that were limited to the client population connected to the practice course in which they were enrolled. The comparison group did not participate in a service experience. All participants completed a demographic survey and a pretest/posttest consisting of a semantic differential (SD) and the Community Service Involvement Preference Inventory (CSIPI). Each member of the experimental group was also required to keep a journal. Analysis of variance of the concepts and factors of the SD showed minimal change, however, descriptive analysis of the adjective pairs did indicate a change in attitude occurred. Analysis of variance of the phases and factors of the CSIPI indicated no movement through phases but qualitative analysis of the journal entries, using the Service Learning Model, indicated developmental differences between experimental groups existed. The younger students in this study consistently showed more statistical and descriptive activity than did the older students. This activity was concrete and in the direction of a more heightened awareness of and commitment to community. The older students had already been participating in service learning for three years. They did not show as much of a difference from the beginning to the end of the study as the younger students but, they did exhibit more sophistication and maturity in their reflections and a more abstract view of community issues and needs. Overall, results indicated that the descriptive analyses produced more informative data than the inferential analyses. The data strongly suggests that design and implementation of service learning programs should consider using developmental learning models to clarify expectations and maximize positive outcomes.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. While the study clearly focuses on service learning (criterion A) and uses a quantitative evaluation design with pretest/posttest measures and ANOVA (criterion C), the participants are occupational therapy students enrolled in practice courses, i.e., higher education students rather than children in K–12 or equivalent levels. Therefore, criterion B is not met and the study should be excluded from this review on service learning interventions in primary/secondary education.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44411067:**

-- **Title:** 'Interprofessional education and service learning: a model for the future of health professions education'

-- **Abstract**: 'The implementation of interprofessional education for healthcare professionals has been lackluster, at best, since it was recommended by the Institute of Medicine. There have been various attempts in institutions of higher learning to meet this goal with mixed results. Herbert Wertheim College of Medicine, Florida International University has developed the Green Family NeighborhoodHELP (TM) (GFNHelp) program to meet this challenge. GFNHelp is an interprofessional, longitudinal, service-learning program for healthcare students. Through participation in this program medical students team up with students from other professions, such as nursing, social work, and law, and collaborate to improve health outcomes for medically underserved families in the community. This educational program emphasizes the Core Competencies of the Interprofessional Education Collaborative through community-based service-learning, allowing student teams to engage firsthand and address the impact of social determinants on health.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. While the study clearly focuses on a service-learning program (Green Family NeighborhoodHELP) satisfying criterion A, the participants are healthcare students in higher education (medical, nursing, social work, law), not children in K–12 or equivalent, so criterion B is not met. In addition, the abstract does not describe a quantitative evaluation design or report quantitative outcomes; it appears to be a descriptive/program description article rather than a quantitative evaluation study, so criterion C is also not met. Therefore, the study should be excluded from the review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44252654:**

-- **Title:** 'The Importance of Developing Communication Skills Perceptions of Dental Hygiene Students.'

-- **Abstract**: 'Purpose: The purpose of this study was to gather data from first- and second-year dental hygiene students concerning their perceptions of the benefits and possible impediments to effective patient communication. Additionally, the students were asked to theorize as to the impact emerging communication technologies could have on oral health promotion, practice administration and patient/provider communication. Methods: A self-administered questionnaire of 6 open-ended queries was employed. Thematic analysis was conducted to reveal themes related to their perceived ability to effectively communicate, perceived barriers to communication, possible solutions to lessen or eliminate these barriers, and the impact of emerging technologies on interpersonal communication. Results: The questionnaire was completed by 63 of 68 students (93%). Patient apathy and patient unwillingness to change detrimental health-related habits were the most frequently cited barriers to effective communication. Of the students having patient contact, many stated that they were less sure of their ability to communicate effectively if the patient differed from themselves, such as being elderly or being from another culture. While most of the students believed their fundamental communication skills were good, many noted that improving their higher-order skills, such as conveying empathy or displaying a nonjudgmental attitude, were essential to being more effective communicators. Many students felt emerging technologies such as universal translators could potentially assist them in overcoming some of their perceived deficiencies. Conclusion: While perceived inadequacies will likely diminish as the students gain more experience in school and later in private practice, dental hygiene programs may wish to consider implementing additional structured educational experiences to better prepare students to address patient apathy and to effectively convey a sense of personal compassion. Promoting student involvement in community outreach activities and providing a variety of service learning opportunities, including foreign travel, may broaden student experiences and deepen their awareness and appreciation of verbal and nonverbal communications displayed by differing cultures.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. The study participants are dental hygiene students in a professional training program, not children in K–12 education. The abstract does not indicate that these are equivalent to primary or secondary school students. Additionally, the study is qualitative in nature, using open-ended questions and thematic analysis, and does not report quantitative evaluation outcomes. Although the conclusion mentions service learning opportunities as a recommendation, the study itself is not an evaluation of a specific service learning intervention. Therefore, it does not meet inclusion criteria B (K–12 population) or C (quantitative evaluation of an intervention).

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44258772:**

-- **Title:** 'A Phenomenological Approach to Examining Perceptions of Middle School Students and Their Educators about Their Service-learning Experiences'

-- **Abstract**: 'A primary purpose of public education is to ensure that all citizens have the knowledge, skills, and dispositions to participate in a democracy. Service learning is a democratic, constructivist, instructional strategy that connects academic learning with community needs. This phenomenological study described: (a) how youth, their educators, and community partners who experience service learning perceive its effects on themselves, their schools, and their community lives and (b) how professional-development opportunities need to be structured in order to encourage high quality civics education instruction. The problem this study addressed was how to solve youth civic and political disengagement. This study relied heavily upon in-depth interviews of nine middle school students, six middle school educators, and one community partner from a rural community in the northeastern United States. In addition, participant journals and grant documents were reviewed. Rubin and Rubin s guidelines and Tesh s eight step coding process were used to analyze each transcript, journal, and document. Results showed that service learning promoted the application of academic content, motivated students, appealed to various learning styles, built relationships, encouraged collaboration, and provided leadership opportunities. By better understanding the benefits and challenges of service learning projects as described by participants, this study could facilitate positive social change and improvements to current civic education instruction, including higher quality service-learning projects. By working collaboratively, youth, educators, and community partners can address the problem of youth disengagement from civic and political life, thus ensuring the continuation of a healthy democracy.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The study clearly focuses on service learning and involves middle school students (grades within K-12), so criteria A and B are met. However, the design is explicitly described as a phenomenological study using in-depth interviews, participant journals, and document review, with qualitative coding and analysis (Rubin & Rubin; Tesh’s eight-step coding process). There is no indication of quantitative evaluation, numeric outcomes, or statistical analysis. Therefore, criterion C (quantitative evaluation study) is not met, and the study should be excluded from the review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44410626:**

-- **Title:** 'Joining the Global Village: Teaching Globalization with Wikipedia'

-- **Abstract**: 'This paper presents an analysis of my experiences with a teaching activity that engages students in publishing in Wikipedia on issues relating to globalization. It begins with a short overview of some of the current debates revolving around teaching globalization, which lay ground for the assignment. I discuss how this teaching tool fits with a number of dimensions related to teaching globalization, particularly with an international service learning approach. Finally, I present assessment data from a series of surveys I conducted from 2008 to 2016 on the effectiveness of the assignment.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The study describes a teaching activity involving Wikipedia and globalization, framed partly as international service learning. However, the context is clearly higher education (surveys from 2008–2016 of students in a university course), not primary or secondary school (K–12). Therefore, inclusion criterion B (participants must be K–12 students or equivalent) is not met. Even though there appears to be an evaluation component (surveys on effectiveness), the population mismatch is sufficient to exclude the study from this K–12-focused review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44261059:**

-- **Title:** 'Groupwork course experiences: An alternate pedagogical approach'

-- **Abstract**: 'Numerous concerns exist in the groupwork and counseling literatures regarding the requirement that students participate in experiential counseling groups as part of their academic coursework. This research examined the use of service learning task groups as an alternate way to address this required component of group counseling coursework to address multiple relationship concerns between faculty and students. Results indicated that while many group counseling skills were demonstrated in the task group, not all skills manifested. Group skills that were more  safe  in terms of counselor risk were demonstrated more often. Less  safe  or risky group skills for the counselor, such as providing feedback to a group member, were demonstrated less frequently. The authors offer implications for research and practice. (PsycINFO Database Record (c) 2016 APA, all rights reserved)'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The study focuses on service learning task groups within group counseling coursework in higher education. Participants are students in counseling programs, not children in K-12 or equivalent primary/secondary education. Although it appears to be an evaluation of a pedagogical intervention and thus may involve quantitative or mixed methods, it fails the key inclusion criterion regarding participant age/educational level. Therefore, it should be excluded from the review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44252994:**

-- **Title:** 'Integrating objectives for clinical education, research, and service learning into community health promotion projects.'

-- **Abstract**: 'Background and Purpose. Health promotion has become an important focus of professional physical therapist education. The combination of service learning, clinical education, and research presents a unique opportunity for students to develop the full professional role of the physical therapist. The purpose of this paper is to describe a community-based health promotion pro-gram that meets this full range of objectives. Students develop, implement, and evaluate a program that meets the health needs of an identified population within the community, using education, screening, and various intervention strategies. The project is carried out across 1 full academic year. Faculty and clinical instructors serve as advisors.Method/Model Description and Evaluation. A fall course covers the full scope of the project, including conducting a needs assessment and developing a proposal to cover the program s theoretical foundation, goals and objectives, educational and therapeutic interventions, an evaluation plan, budgeting, and marketing. Students carry out the programs over an 8-week period in the spring semester and evaluate it in a follow-up session in the summer. Students complete a final written report that is presented to the community partner with recommendations for new initiatives or iniproved procedures. Students deliver oral presentations in a public forum.Outcomes. Several stakeholders, including students, faculty, and community partners, all benefit from these programs. Students gain confidence in leadership and design of health promotion programs as a facet of clinical education and professional practice; projects can contribute to faculty scholar ship and community networks; community partners take advantage of needed pro Bono services.Discussion and Conclusion. Health pro motion programs can be an important component of clinical education, providing opportunities for practice in community settings. The projects address objectives related to research, evidence-based practice, education, administration, marketing, and consultation.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. Although the article clearly focuses on service learning (criterion A met), the participants are professional physical therapist students in clinical education, not children in K–12 or equivalent school settings, so criterion B is not met. In addition, the paper is a descriptive report of a community-based health promotion/service learning project rather than a quantitative evaluation study; no quantitative design, comparison, or statistical outcomes are reported, so criterion C is not met. Therefore the study does not fit the inclusion criteria for the review on service learning interventions in primary/secondary education.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44251865:**

-- **Title:** 'Makers &amp; ShaPers: A Chicken Coop in St. Louis.'

-- **Abstract**: 'The article relates the success story of the Maplewood Richmond Heights District in Saint Louis, Missouri which is composed of an early childhood center and elementary, middle and high schools. Particular focus is given to the importance of hands-on, creation-based learning to the Maplewood curriculum. According to high school service learning teacher Patrice Bryan, the work of students have a positive impact on their community. Also mentioned is the circulation growth of Maplewood s library.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The article describes a district-wide emphasis on hands-on, creation-based learning and mentions a high school service learning teacher and positive community impact, but it does not clearly report a distinct service learning intervention being evaluated quantitatively. The abstract reads as a descriptive or success-story narrative, with no indication of quantitative outcomes, measures, or study design. Although the setting is K–12, criterion C (quantitative evaluation study) is not met. Therefore, the study should be excluded.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44257455:**

-- **Title:** 'Building Migratory Bridges'

-- **Abstract**: 'The Building Migratory Bridges (BOMB) program--a collaboration between the Marvel wood School and Audubon Sharon in Connecticut and Conservation Research Education Action (CR EA), a U.S. not-for-profit in Panama--uses nontropical migratory bird research in the United States and Panama to demonstrate how negative environmental impacts in one country can have harmful consequences in others. This article discusses the BOMB program in terms of student-learning and community-service opportunities it presents. It further suggests that more hands-on science programs that analyze root causes of critical global issues are needed if students are to comprehend and tackle world environmental challenges. (Contains 6 figures.)'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The study describes the Building Migratory Bridges (BOMB) program, highlighting its student-learning and community-service opportunities and arguing for more hands-on science programs. However, the abstract does not indicate that this is a quantitative evaluation study (no mention of research design, data collection, quantitative outcomes, or analyses). It appears to be a descriptive or conceptual article about the program rather than an empirical quantitative evaluation. While the participants are likely K-12 students and the program involves service and learning components, criterion C (quantitative evaluation study) is not met. Therefore, the study should be excluded.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44256700:**

-- **Title:** 'Graduate Students Serve Extension as Evaluation Consultants'

-- **Abstract**: 'In an effort to provide graduate students at a distance with field-based learning experiences and evaluation resources to statewide Extension programs, 24 Master s students participating in a distance-delivered program evaluation course served as evaluation consultants for Extension programs. State evaluation specialists unable to conduct face-to-face trainings with local Extension agents because of budget constraints should consider mentoring and allowing trained graduate students to assist with local program evaluation efforts. The graduate students benefit from an eager audience and a real-world setting in which to apply their evaluation skills, while their clientele benefit by acquiring individually tailored program-specific evaluation resources.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. The study describes graduate students (Master's level) serving as evaluation consultants for Extension programs. The participants are higher-education students, not children in primary/secondary (K–12) education, so criterion B is not met. In addition, the abstract does not indicate that this is a quantitative evaluation study of a service-learning intervention’s outcomes; rather, it is a descriptive account or practice note. Therefore, the study does not meet the review inclusion criteria.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44254233:**

-- **Title:** 'Pre-Service Physical Education Teachers  Indigenous Knowledge, Cultural Competency and Pedagogy: A Service Learning Intervention'

-- **Abstract**: 'In this paper, we investigate the effects of a community- and school-based service learning experience (SLE) on pre-service physical education teachers  Indigenous knowledge, cultural competency and pedagogy. Informed by the theoretical tenets of Indigenous research methodologies, experiential learning and critical reflection, we examine 55 final-year pre-service physical education teachers  (age: 21.9 [8.3]; 10.7% low SES; 68% females) cultural learning and competency in a core unit of study with a six-week SLE. Measures of their experiences included reflective journals, multicultural teaching competency scales and focus group interviews. Findings support the design of the SLE, with statistically significant changes in pre-service teachers  perceptions of their cultural competency (p &lt; 0.001). Pre-service teachers were able to challenge their assumptions about Indigenous students, plan and implement student-centred and culturally relevant pedagogies. Attention is drawn to the design of this SLE and demonstrates the importance of using Indigenous community members and teachers as mentors, which has not been explored in previous SLE studies.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. While the study clearly examines a service learning experience (SLE) and uses quantitative measures (multicultural teaching competency scales with statistical analysis), the participants are pre-service physical education teachers (final-year, mean age ~22), i.e., university students, not K–12 pupils. The review is restricted to service learning interventions where the participants are children in primary/secondary education (K–12 or equivalent). Therefore, criterion B is not met and the study should be excluded.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44262854:**

-- **Title:** 'Cultural Conflict and Gender Socialization as Obstacles to High School Graduation: Some Insights from Samoan Adolescent Women'

-- **Abstract**: 'Cultural &amp; gender socialization factors contributing to the poor academic performance of adolescent Samoan females (Fs) were examined through review of school records &amp; survey of 12 American Samoan Fs ages 14-19, attending public high school in HI. Samoan parents had high educational expectations for their children &amp; high regard for education s value as a vehicle for occupational &amp; social advancement. However, Samoan cultural practicies -- ranging from disdain for showing off &amp; competition to emphasis on learning by rote &amp; experiential learning -- conflicted with the behaviors needed for success &amp; the atmosphere of the educational system. Likewise, gender socialization practices of young Samoan Fs, particularly their responsibilities in caring for younger children, undermined opportunities to succeed in school. The Samoan Fs attempted to cope with the conflict between school &amp; culture &amp; socialization in typical Samoan avoidance of confrontation, by running away from school or home, using drugs or alcohol, &amp; indulgence in sexual activity. 24 References. D. Generoli'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. The study examines cultural conflict and gender socialization factors that contribute to poor academic performance among Samoan adolescent females through school record review and a survey. There is no indication that the study involves a service learning intervention or any structured service learning program; rather, it is an observational/descriptive study of cultural and gender socialization influences on schooling. Although the participants are high school students (within K–12) and the study uses quantitative data, criterion A (focus on service learning) is not met, so the study should be excluded from the review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44259284:**

-- **Title:** 'A Qualitative Case Study: Examining the Social and Cultural Impact of SISTERS Club'

-- **Abstract**: 'Adolescent girls face social and identity issues as they transition into adulthood and after-school programs are one venue that allows adults to help students deal with the difficulties encountered. This is a qualitative case study focused on the impact the SISTERS Club, an after-school program, had on a diverse group of middle school female students over the course of a five year period, from 2001 to 2006. This study described the impact on identity development, multicultural relationships, and the long-term impact of the after-school program on the female adolescents interviewed. I investigated the racial, social, and cultural barriers that impacted the development of adolescent girls and how a strong after-school program brought about positive identity growth and feelings of “belonging” to a gender specific group. I began by investigating the social constructs of gender and race that are prevalent to adolescent girls. I also investigated the impact of mentoring and service learning on the identity development of participants. Finally, I investigated the social and cultural impact the SISTERS Club program had on the girls involved and how this type of program can be utilized in other urban communities.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The study involves middle school female students (K-12 population) and mentions mentoring and service learning within an after-school program, so criteria A and B are met. However, it is explicitly described as a qualitative case study and does not report a quantitative evaluation of the intervention. Because the inclusion criteria require the report to be a quantitative evaluation study, this study does not meet criterion C and should be excluded from the review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44263596:**

-- **Title:** 'Teaching More Than English: Connecting ESL Students to Their Community Through Services Learning.'

-- **Abstract**: 'The article discusses the benefits of service learning for English language learner (ELL) students. Service learning can help ELL students integrate into the larger community and student body, something that tends to be unusual as ELL students are segregated in many of their classes. The author describes a project his class did, creating a Spanish/English phrase book for the community. The project helped students become part of the school and work outside their usual peer groups as well as provide all the students with a sense of purpose in their work.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The study clearly focuses on service learning (criterion A) and involves school-based ESL/ELL students, who are likely within the K–12 range (criterion B is probably met). However, the description indicates this is a descriptive or reflective article about a class project, outlining perceived benefits and describing an example activity. There is no indication of a quantitative evaluation design, data collection, or analysis (e.g., no mention of measures, sample sizes, statistical results). Therefore it does not meet criterion C (quantitative evaluation study) and should be excluded from the review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44259843:**

-- **Title:** 'Service-learning enhances physical therapy students’ ability to examine fall risk in older adults'

-- **Abstract**: 'Service-learning (SL) is one educational methodology that provides students opportunities to practice and refine affective, cognitive and psychomotor skills in a community-based setting. Purpose: The aims of this study were: 1) to investigate the impact of SL on physical therapy (PT) students  attitudes and perceived clinical competence when working with older adults, and 2) to evaluate the difference between perceptions of students who developed and implemented the SL activity vs those who implemented only. Methods: Eighty PT students, (from two consecutive cohorts) enrolled in a first-year geriatrics course, participated in this study. The first cohort designed and implemented the SL activities, while the second cohort only implemented these activities. Student self-perceived anxiety, confidence, knowledge and skills were assessed by pre- and post-SL surveys using a 5-point Likert-like scale. Results: Both cohorts reported similar anxiety and confidence levels pre-SL. For both cohorts, with the exception of one item, all responses to anxiety items significantly decreased from pre- to post-SL. All students  confidence levels for assessing and mitigating fall risk in older adults increased post-SL (p &lt; 0.01). Moreover, students in cohort 1, who designed and delivered SL activities, expressed self-perceived improvement in their ability to interpret results of evaluations, to determine type and severity of balance impairments, and to serve a geriatric population (p &lt; 0.05) compared to students in cohort 2 who only implemented the activities. Conclusion: Embedding SL into a geriatrics course decreased self-perceived anxiety and improved student confidence regarding working with older adults. Also, empowering students to be actively involved in the design and implementation of SL increased self-perceived ability in interpreting results from assessments. (PsycINFO Database Record (c) 2018 APA, all rights reserved)'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. While the study clearly focuses on a service-learning intervention and uses quantitative pre–post survey methods, the participants are physical therapy students enrolled in a first-year geriatrics course (i.e., higher education/university level). The review eligibility criteria restrict participants to children in primary/secondary education (K–12 or equivalent). Therefore, criterion B is not met and the study should be excluded.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44254290:**

-- **Title:** 'The  Real-World  Experience: Students  Perspectives on Service-Learning Projects'

-- **Abstract**: 'The purpose of this paper is to identify how students gain  real-world  experience via service-learning projects. This article describes the results of a pilot study conducted with over 75 business graduate students to investigate the effects of service-learning curriculum at the collegiate level. The following qualitative data was collected: observations, field notes, interviews, video-taped group meetings, and student reflection journals. This data was collected to gain insight on the research question,  What are business students? views of service-learning projects and how they contribute to  real-world? experience?  In addition, this article briefly discusses literature on the ethnographic qualitative approach and its significance, as well as the service-learning literature that continues to evolve as the instigation of such projects continues to draw the attention of researchers and educators.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude the study because it does not meet key inclusion criteria. Although it focuses on service-learning (criterion A satisfied), the participants are business graduate students at the collegiate level, not children in K-12 or equivalent (criterion B not satisfied). Additionally, the study is explicitly qualitative, using observations, field notes, interviews, video-taped meetings, and reflection journals, rather than a quantitative evaluation design (criterion C not satisfied). Therefore, it should be excluded from the systematic review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44253498:**

-- **Title:** 'Occupational therapy communications, and industry: partners for learning and OT service delivery.'

-- **Abstract**: 'Lucinda Dale describes a collaboration between occupational therapy and communications students to offer consultation services to local industries.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. The study describes a collaboration between occupational therapy and communications students to provide consultation services to local industries. The participants are clearly university-level students, not children in K–12 education. Therefore, inclusion criterion B (participants must be in K–12 or equivalent) is not met. Additionally, there is no indication that this is a quantitative evaluation study (criterion C), and while it may involve a form of service learning, failure to meet the participant age/grade criterion is sufficient for exclusion.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44409974:**

-- **Title:** 'Reflections on the Dental Pipeline Program s Efforts Regarding Community-Based Dental Education'

-- **Abstract**: 'Although many changes are being made, many are still needed in educating future dental professionals about prevention programs, ethics, professionalism, communication, cultural competence, and access to care. It is not yet clear if producing more dentists will put these professionals into underserved areas where they are needed, with the skills they need. The achievements of the Pipeline. Profession, and Practice: Community-Based Dental Education program schools in increasing the amount of time students spend in community-based education and service-learning programs result from a widespread, national effort to meet this challenge. This-article is a personal reflection and review of these programs, considering what has been learned and still needs to be learned about student educational impact and quality, faculty issues, program management. infrastructure and logistics, and financial impact, with suggestions for future study that can help dental schools design the most effective programs.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The article focuses on community-based dental education and service-learning programs within dental schools, i.e., among dental students who are tertiary-level (postsecondary) learners, not children in grades K–12 or their European equivalents. Thus, criterion B (participants are K–12 students) is not met. In addition, the abstract describes the article as a personal reflection and review of programs, discussing what has been and still needs to be learned, rather than reporting a quantitative evaluation study with empirical data, so criterion C is also not met. Although the content does relate to service-learning, failure to meet the population and study design criteria means the study should be excluded from the review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44263675:**

-- **Title:** 'In the service of citizenship.'

-- **Abstract**: 'Comments on social responsibility among students, focusing on the role of education as source of citizenship preparation. What is reflected by higher education; Comparison of community service and service learning; Analysis of John Dewey s philosophical work.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The article focuses on social responsibility and citizenship preparation, discussing higher education, community service vs. service learning, and Dewey’s philosophy. There is no indication that the study involves K–12 (primary/secondary) students; instead, it explicitly refers to higher education. Furthermore, the text appears to be a conceptual or theoretical discussion, not a quantitative evaluation of a service learning intervention with empirical data. Therefore, it fails inclusion criteria B (K–12 participants) and C (quantitative evaluation study) and should be excluded from the review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44255658:**

-- **Title:** 'International Service and Public Health Learning Objectives for Medical Students'

-- **Abstract**: 'Objective: We aimed to improve the education of medical students involved in a longitudinal perinatal health improvement project in Gowa, Malawi. Design: We conducted qualitative interviews with students who participated in the project, reviewed their quantitative reports, and assessed the application of methodologies consonant with the learning objectives of a novel community health improvement course within their experience. Setting: The Gowa Health Promotions Project, designed to improve perinatal care for women and their families within the Gowa Health Clinic, used community participatory research strategies. Method: Medical students partnered with clinic workers and the local residents evaluated and revised an existing perinatal educational programme. Qualitative and quantitative health and programme data were collected, and programme revisions were implemented. The value of the student experiences as a public health educational tool was evaluated by the authors. Results: Project sustainability was enhanced by a fellowship and planning for sequential students. The community health course structure and goals enhanced learning in the project. Engagement of investigators as early as possible in an international public health enhancement project improves student learning and ongoing commitment. Conclusion: Service learning objectives aimed at providing valuable medical learning to student learners immersed in other cultures are consistent with evidence-based learning objectives in the field of public health. Proactively structuring this experience to explicate these goals can enhance student learning. This dual strategy may improve the sustainability of international health programmes by educating medical students while leading them into careers where these skills will be leveraged.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. Although the study is clearly about service learning (international public health/service objectives for medical students), the participants are university-level medical students rather than K–12 pupils, so criterion B is not satisfied. Additionally, the primary design is qualitative (interviews, narrative evaluation of learning objectives) and not a quantitative evaluation of outcomes, so criterion C is also not met. Therefore, the study does not fit the inclusion criteria for this review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44255545:**

-- **Title:** 'The Gift Economy of Elite Schooling: The Changing Contours and Contradictions of Privileged Benefaction'

-- **Abstract**: 'Privileged benefaction in elite schools and the moral dilemmas, contradictions and power politics involved are the focus of this paper. The notion of  the gift  provides our analytical lens. We concentrate on two girls  schools--one in South Africa and one in England. These were both built, in various ways, on the British model of public schooling. These schools pride themselves on their gifting practices. We offer a broad overview of the different local and global manifestations of and justifications for these practices as they occur in these schools and in the transnational organisations of which the schools are members.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. The paper analyzes privileged benefaction and gifting practices in elite girls' schools in South Africa and England using an analytical/theoretical lens. It does not describe or evaluate a service learning intervention, does not present a quantitative evaluation design or outcomes, and appears to be a qualitative/analytic study of school practices and politics rather than an intervention study with K-12 student participants. Therefore it fails criteria A (service learning focus) and C (quantitative evaluation study).

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44257587:**

-- **Title:** 'When You Are Needed: PA s Summer of Service Program.'

-- **Abstract**: 'Summer of Service, a national summer service learning program for early adolescents is described. The three tenets of this program--intentional design, youth empowerment, and the opportunity to be of use--combine to make a powerful learning experience. Student input is involved at every stage of the program, and learning is incorporated through a reflection process. (TD)'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The study clearly focuses on a service learning program (Summer of Service) and participants are early adolescents, which likely corresponds to middle school grades (within K-12). However, the abstract describes the program and its principles (intentional design, youth empowerment, reflection) but does not indicate that it is a quantitative evaluation study or that any quantitative outcomes were measured or analyzed. It appears to be a descriptive or conceptual piece rather than an empirical quantitative evaluation. Therefore, it does not meet inclusion criterion C and should be excluded from the review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44258306:**

-- **Title:** 'The Integration of Academic and Vocational Education. System Design.'

-- **Abstract**: 'The literature on integration of academic and vocational education was reviewed to identify strategies for school districts planning to integrate their academic and vocational programs. Among the topics examined were the following: reasons for integrating academic and vocational education; meaning of curriculum integration; essential components of integrated programs; forms and examples of curriculum integration; effectiveness of various curriculum integration efforts; and policy questions entailed in integrating academic and vocational programs. After the literature review had been completed, an instructional guidance system was proposed as a starting point for school divisions planning to integrate academic and vocational education. The proposal called for the following: curriculum of integrated academic and vocational education that is organized around academic and technical skills and personal qualities and delivered through situated learning; interdisciplinary approach in both academic and vocational classrooms; collaboration between vocational and academic teachers to devise thematic units/projects/assessments; curriculum situated in both school- and work-based learning environments; and appropriate connecting activities, including career counseling and service learning, to link both environments. (Contains 20 references. Also included is a 59-item annotated bibliography.) (MN)'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. The article is a literature review and system design proposal about integrating academic and vocational education. It is not a primary quantitative evaluation study of an intervention. Service learning is only mentioned as one of several possible connecting activities, and no specific service learning intervention with K–12 student participants is quantitatively evaluated. Therefore it fails criterion C (quantitative evaluation study) and does not clearly meet criterion A (focus specifically on service learning), so it should be excluded.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44250920:**

-- **Title:** 'Implementation of Service-Learning in Business Education: Issues and Challenges.'

-- **Abstract**: 'This paper examines the issues and challenges in the implementation of service-learning in undergraduate business education. It also provides an assessment of the students  learning efficacy and outcomes over time through the service-learning participation. Service-learning is a pedagogical approach that integrates academic learning and community service. It involves partnership among various stakeholders like students, faculty members, community members, and social agencies. There are challenges for various stakeholders to implement service-learning in business courses. The findings of a survey of a group of business students participated in service-learning projects show that the students have an increased level of sense of social responsibility, ethical and moral behavior after the participation in service-learning projects. Nevertheless, no significant difference is found for learning outcomes between the pre-test and post-test. The paper addresses the critical success factors and constraints of the integration of service-learning and provides recommendations for designing experiential service-learning projects in business education. [ABSTRACT FROM AUTHOR] Copyright of Journal of Teaching in International Business is the property of Taylor &amp; Francis Ltd and its content may not be copied or emailed to multiple sites or posted to a listserv without the copyright holder s express written permission. However, users may print, download, or email articles for individual use. This abstract may be abridged. No warranty is given about the accuracy of the copy. Users should refer to the original published version of the material for the full abstract. (Copyright applies to all Abstracts.)'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. While the study clearly focuses on service-learning (criterion A) and appears to report quantitative evaluation of outcomes (pre-test/post-test, survey findings; criterion C), the participants are undergraduate business students, not children in primary or secondary education (K–12 or equivalent). Therefore, it fails criterion B and should be excluded from the review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44258467:**

-- **Title:** 'The Impact of Experiential Learning in a Service-learning Context from the Adult Learners  Perspective: A Phenomenological Inquiry'

-- **Abstract**: 'In higher education, numerous experiential learning programs are offered to enhance students’ learning, including international travel programs, immersion programs, internship programs, and service-learning programs. Although students participating in these programs are each higher education institution’s number one stakeholders, rarely are they asked about the impact of these programs on their learning, both personally and professionally. For future graduate students, higher education institutions, program designers, and community partners, understanding the perspectives of graduate alumni that have participated in experiential learning programs can be valuable for the future development, assessment, and improvement of such programs. For this reason, the purpose of this qualitative phenomenological study was to describe the impact of experiential learning from the perspectives of graduate alumni of Pepperdine Graziadio Business School (PGBS) who completed the Master of Science in Management and Leadership (MSML) Education to Community (E2C) service-learning capstone project. The study was guided by research questions that addressed graduate alumni strategies and practices when leading a change initiative in a service-learning context, the challenges they faced, their sense of the personal and professional significance of the opportunity, the lessons learned, and their recommendations for future programs. The goal of the study was to deliver to program designers current research that might contribute to the continued development and success of the MSML program. Altogether, through data collection and data analysis, the findings fully supported the effectiveness of the program as expressed from the perspectives of graduate alumni related to student satisfaction and learning outcomes. The impact as described by graduate alumni indicated positive outcomes and strong agreement of the immediate and continued benefits of their involvement in the E2C service-learning capstone project. The graduate alumni recounted that the opportunity to learn and apply theory by participating in the capstone project, with the support of faculty-to-student coaching and peer-to-peer mentoring led to long-lasting impacts, both personally and professionally. Of note, the findings suggested that in general graduate alumni gained a greater awareness of the non-profit sector, established relationships, developed leadership responsibilities, determined strategies and practices for leading change, and experienced personal development, and professional advancement. Because of these findings, a couple of specific implications are suggested for future MSML graduate students and current MSML program designers. Future graduate students interested in getting the most out of their E2C capstone service-learning project can incorporate the learning strategies, based on the successful experiences of graduate alumni, which include: (a) the utilization of MSML program resources, (b) academic collaboration, and (c) community partner collaboration. Additionally, a particular implication for program designers includes the application of a revised version of the collaborative approach to teaching students how to lead change model. The four components of this simple model create a platform for students to thrive in leading a change initiative through an E2C service-learning capstone project. The model consists of four primary components: (a) theory, (b) application, (c) coaching, and (d) evaluation.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: The study focuses on a service-learning capstone project, satisfying criterion A. However, the participants are graduate alumni of a Master of Science in Management and Leadership program at a higher education business school, not children in primary or secondary education (K–12), so criterion B is not met. In addition, the study is explicitly described as a qualitative phenomenological inquiry, not a quantitative evaluation, so criterion C is not met. Because two of the required inclusion criteria (K–12 population and quantitative evaluation) are not satisfied, this study should be excluded from the review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44251492:**

-- **Title:** 'Four typologies of communication activism pedagogy.'

-- **Abstract**: 'The article discusses several typologies of communication activism pedagogy (CAP). Topics discussed include ways teachers of communication incorporates the sense of social justice; participation of students civic engagement projects through classrooms and curricula; and suggests that teachers of communication need to stop looking off-campus for civic engagement projects.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. The article discusses typologies of communication activism pedagogy in general and how teachers incorporate social justice and civic engagement projects. There is no indication that the participants are K–12 students; it appears to concern communication teachers and their students in a higher-education context. Additionally, the abstract does not describe a quantitative evaluation study of an intervention, but rather a conceptual/pedagogical discussion. While civic engagement projects are mentioned, the abstract does not clearly indicate a structured service learning intervention with K–12 participants or a quantitative assessment of outcomes. Therefore, it does not meet inclusion criteria A, B, and C.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44263789:**

-- **Title:** 'Coordinating Physics and Education Instruction.'

-- **Abstract**: 'This article describes a university science course that integrates physics, education, and community outreach. The class both improves student mastery of science and teaching as well as addresses the university s mission to pursue research, education, and community service. The structure, implementation, and effectiveness of the course are discussed to present on approach or model for science education. [ABSTRACT FROM AUTHOR] Copyright of Journal of College Science Teaching is the property of National Science Teachers Association and its content may not be copied or emailed to multiple sites or posted to a listserv without the copyright holder s express written permission. However, users may print, download, or email articles for individual use. This abstract may be abridged. No warranty is given about the accuracy of the copy. Users should refer to the original published version of the material for the full abstract. (Copyright applies to all Abstracts.)'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. Although the course includes community outreach and may resemble service learning, the participants are university students rather than K–12 pupils, so criterion B (K–12 population) is not met. The study appears to be about higher education science instruction and outreach, not a service learning intervention implemented with primary/secondary school students.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44262385:**

-- **Title:** 'Teaching Data Reproducibility through Service Learning'

-- **Abstract**: 'Data reproducibility is becoming increasingly important in the social sciences, but it has yet to be incorporated into many undergraduate sociology programs. This note describes a service-learning activity that can be added to an introductory statistics course. Students partner with a nonprofit and analyze quantitative data to answer questions selected by the agency. Reproducibility is the central mechanism of communication between the nonprofit, the students, and the course instructor. An assessment of the project suggests that students achieve an understanding of how to create reproducible data. They also come to see its value as a method of communication about data decisions.'

-- **Answer (GPT)**: Exclude. Although the study clearly focuses on a service-learning activity (criterion A), the participants are undergraduate students in an introductory statistics course, not children in primary or secondary education (K–12 or equivalent), so criterion B is not met. The abstract also does not clearly indicate that this is a formal quantitative evaluation study; it only mentions an assessment of the project without specifying quantitative outcomes or methods, so criterion C is also uncertain. Failure to meet the population criterion is sufficient for exclusion from this K–12-focused review.

-- **Answer (GPT - numeric)**: 0

-- **Answer (Human)**: 0

*Please add a comment on whether and why you agree with the GPT decision or not:*

&nbsp;

-- **Model**: gpt-5.1-2025-11-13

-- **Run date**: 2026-06-04

&nbsp;

**STUDY-ID: 44259793:**

-- **Title:** 'Collaboration, teamwork and service learning within an after school community arts project: After school mural arts project'

-- **Abstract**: 'Students learn better when they can see, touch and visualize.
